data.title: SOXL: Time To Cash In
data.description:
As the name suggests, the Direxion Daily Semiconductor Bull 3X Shares ETF (SOXL) is a 3x leveraged bet on the Semiconductor Index (SOX). That makes it a risky bet, but as this article highlights, some periods—like - like now - are riskier than others, and it is essential to have an exit strategy.

SOXL Overview

SOXL is a leveraged bet on the NYSE Semiconductor Index. The index tracks the thirty largest U.S.-listed semiconductor companies and has a modified market capitalization weighting system, which means Nvidia (NVDA) is not always the top holding.

Slight differences in weighting are perhaps not the main consideration. SOXL is essentially a bet on the semiconductor industry group as a whole, and the iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) is a good proxy. This shows semis have been trending strongly higher.

Reasons to Be Cautious

I'm not in the "AI is a bubble" camp, but I do think semi stocks are stretched here after a 130% rally from last April's lows. The PE ratio (TTM) of 44 is high for an industry group by any standards.

Sure, it could expand even further, and I would not say "sell" on valuation alone. But there are other reasons to be cautious. The first comes from the SOXX chart above. The rally has very nearly reached the top of the blue channel. Also, if we look at the chart of SOXL, it has nearly reached the top of its trading range.

You don't have to be a technical analysis expert to see where support and resistance are on this chart.

The chart also highlights the risks and rewards - both the 2022 and 2025 declines were around 90% in size. The 2025 rally is 750% and counting.

In terms of risk and reward, the current price of $60.75 seems to offer a lot of risk but limited reward. Sure, it might get slightly higher, but another 750% rally from here is highly unlikely.

You may think semiconductors look strong, and the industry tailwinds look set to continue in 2026. I wouldn't disagree, but the 90% corrections shown above were not triggered by anything sector-specific. 2022 was due to inflation and an aggressive hiking cycle by the Fed. 2025 was due to tariffs.

The Need for an Exit

SOXL can be a great trade, and contrary to popular opinion, it can be held medium-term for months, even a year or two, but it is still a trading vehicle rather than a long-term hold. After all, SOXL has not made new highs since 2021. It is therefore essential to have an exit strategy to lock in profits.

The main enemy of a 3x leveraged ETF is negative compounding. When the underlying declines, mathematics works against a recovery, and it is an uphill battle to break even. Here's an example provided by the SEC:

Let’s say that on Day 1, an index starts with a value of $1000 and a leveraged ETF that seeks to double the return of the index starts at $1000. If the index drops by 100 points on Day 1, it has a 10 percent loss and a resulting value of $900. Assuming it achieved its stated objective, the leveraged ETF would therefore drop 20 percent on that day and have an ending value of $800.

On Day 2, if the index rises 10 percent, the index value increases to $990. For the ETF, its value for Day 2 would rise by 20 percent, which means the ETF would have a value of $960.

On both days, the leveraged ETF did exactly what it was supposed to do – it produced daily returns that were two times the daily index returns.

But let’s look at the results over the 2 day period: the index lost 1 percent (it fell from $1000 to $990) while the 2x leveraged ETF lost 4 percent (it fell from $1000 to $960). That means that over the two day period, the ETF's negative returns were 4 times as much as the two-day return of the index instead of 2 times the return.

When the underlying enters a prolonged correction, this process repeats, and it gets harder and harder to recover to the previous peak. It's a serious risk that is worsened by rebalancing and other factors.

Interestingly, in December, the SEC blocked the registration of some new leveraged ETFs and wrote to Direxion (the manager of SOXL) and eight other providers to express its concerns. Some ETFs were proposing 5x leverage, but the SEC seems uncomfortable with anything over 2x.

We write to express concern regarding the registration of exchange-traded funds that seek to provide more than 200% (2x) leveraged exposure to underlying indices or securities

Again, I think SOXL can be an amazing trade, but timing and risk mitigation are everything.

Conclusions

SOXL has been an amazing trade from the 2025 low, but every trade needs an exit, and I think it is now a sensible time to lock in profits. It's certainly not a good time for new entries with stretched valuations, SOXX at the top of its trend channel, and SOXL at the top of its trading range. I rate SOXL a sell.

data.title: Avis Budget: Unattractive With Soft Used Car Prices
data.description:
Shares of Avis Budget Group, Inc. (CAR) have been an excellent performer over the past year, gaining over 40%. Shares surged in early 2025 as the company is likely a winner of the Administration’s tariff policies. All else equal, higher auto tariffs should support used car pricing, providing a windfall for CAR similar to what we saw post-COVID. However, thus far, the benefit to used car pricing has been more modest than hoped, causing shares to reverse some of those gains and undermining the bull case. I last covered shares of Avis Budget in September, downgrading shares to a “Sell,” which has been the right call with CAR falling by 23% since then. With shares approaching my $100-$120 fair value, now is a good time to revisit Avis Budget.

Tariffs Have Not Lifted Used Car Prices Meaningfully

I became more bearish on CAR later in 2025 as it became clearer that new car tariffs were not driving higher used car prices. All else equal, higher new car prices should push some consumers to buy used cars, driving up those prices. However, with trade deals struck with many key auto importers and weaker discretionary income growth, this tailwind has been more muted than initially hoped. As you can see below, used car prices (as measured by the benchmark Manheim index) have barely budged this year, rising just 0.4% in December from a year prior. Price inflation has been nothing like the post-COVID surge, and used car prices remain about 20% below all-time levels.

This flattish performance masks significant dispersion. As you can see below, luxury cars are significantly outperforming compact and midsize cars. In general, the luxury class is more widely imported, creating more of a tariff impact. Luxury buyers also typically have more discretionary income, making their spending more durable even during periods of weaker growth. Rental companies like Avis Budget have fleets more focused around compact and midsize cars with fewer luxury holdings, so it is likely facing an even more muted reselling environment.

Used Cars Are Much Of Avis's Value

I focus so much on used car prices because that really is the core of the value in Avis Budget, with dynamics here significantly outweighing variations in the rental market. Avis has $19.6 billion of vehicles on its balance sheet and $19.2 billion of vehicle debt. This vehicle debt is nonrecourse as CAR issues securitizations that finance its purchases of cars. It maintains the equity in these structures, meaning it earns any upside from higher sales.

Thus far, Avis Budget has not really seen a benefit from used car pricing. Through nine months, CAR has lost $249 million on vehicle sales, actually worse than the $120 million lost in 2024. I expect this to improve, given it has already sold vehicles with higher residual assumptions. Going forward, essentially, each 1% movement in used car prices is worth $196 million or about $5.50/share. Avis also has $1.9 billion of fleet funding capacity, worth about $54 per share, with higher used car prices providing potential upside beyond this, should they materialize.

Rental Demand Is Modest

Activity in its rental business remains muted, though there are signs of improvement. In the company’s third quarter, Avis Budget earned $10.11 per share, which blew past estimates by $2.21 with revenue growing 1% to $3.5 billion. This was the first quarter in two years that Avis reported a revenue increase. While sales growth was modest, vehicle depreciation was down 16%, and operating expenses fell by 4%. Vehicle depreciation was down about $130 million, reflecting benefits from better used car pricing. Adjusted EBITDA was up a solid 11%, thanks to its focus on cost efficiency.

In the Americas, revenue per day was down 3% to $73.23, with vehicle utilization dropping 20bps to 71.3%, speaking to a modest demand environment. I remain very cautious on travel and leisure spending. Hotel occupancy has fallen for nine consecutive months. While overall consumer spending has been fairly resilient, travel is highly discretionary and one of the first areas consumers can cut back on, which appears to be happening. While I expect consumer spending to rise ~2% in 2026, I expect travel spending to underperform this, creating a difficult operating environment for Avis Budget.

Recalls have been a significant headwind, taking 5% of its fleet off the market. Beyond this, the company cannot sell cars while they are on recall, which has limited its ability to monetize its fleet and take advantage of the brief jump in used car prices earlier this year. I expect this pressure to abate over the next year, which may also help support some incremental improvement in fleet utilization, even in a more modest environment.

Avis Budget has $564 million in cash. Importantly, its corporate debt is well-staggered with no maturities until 2027. Still, Avis is a highly levered business with $6 billion of corporate debt. This is separate from its vehicle debt, and it is recourse debt. The company is leveraged over 6x, excluding vehicle debt, which is extremely high. Over the next year, I expect Avis Budget to use some of its incremental fleet debt capacity to support a reduction in corporate borrowings.

Alongside Q3 results, management said that it expects full-year EBITDA to be towards the lower end of guidance, which has been more consistent with my own view. Back in September, I saw there being about $3-$3.5 billion of value in its used cars and CAR generating $800-$900 million of EBITDA, for a fair value of $100-120. I continue to view about ~8x EBITDA plus its residual car value as the appropriate way to value Avis Budget. I now expect 2025 EBITDA of $880-$920 million.

Shares Have Further Downside

I expect rental segment results to be flattish in 2026 relative to 2025, given very modest demand growth, at about $900 million of EBITDA for about $34 of value; assuming 5% growth, could add another $10 of upside. With used car prices showing no momentum, I see closer to $2.5 billion of value here, or no more than $70. This results in a total fair value of $104-$114, still pointing to at least 10% downside from current levels.

As such, I remain a "Sell" on Avis Budget. We are simply not seeing the rise in used car prices once hoped that would support a recovery in shares, and with soft travel spending, its core rental business has limited growth upside as well. Even with shares down 20+% since I last covered them, I see further downside and would continue to avoid shares.

data.title: 3 Encouraging Developments On The Economic Front
data.description:
Recently I penned an article that highlighted the huge disconnect between the equity market that is trading near all-time highs and at valuation levels not seen since the Internet Boom, and dismal consumer sentiment readings.

Consumer activity makes up approximately two thirds of economic activity in the U.S., so this divergence is quite illogical. To put it in perspective, U.S. consumer sentiment was twice as high as it is now at the tail end of the Internet Boom a quarter century ago. All things being equal, one would think that either equities need to have a significant pull back to align with consumer confidence, or the health of the consumer needs to improve significantly to match the optimism of the market.

I have been accused more than once of being a 'permabear'. However, I am just pointing out how extreme valuation levels have become across the market viewed from a historical lens. In a change of pace, in today's column I will highlight several encouraging trends for the consumer and the economy.

#1 - Above 4% GDP Growth:

First, U.S. GDP growth is robust even as it bifurcated and largely dependent on the huge surge of tech spending around the massive AI infrastructure build out going on across the nation. Q2 GDP growth came in at 3.8%, and Q3 GDP growth accelerated to 4.3%. An increase in consumer spending and exports help drive growth above the four percent threshold in the third quarter. And with its latest update last week, the Atlanta Fed's GDPNow has 5.3% GDP growth predicted for Q4.

This is even more impressive considering that growth is not being driven by massive boosts to the money supply, Covid stimulus measures and legislation that pumped trillions more into the economy via more spending for things like infrastructure that occurred following the pandemic. And U.S. growth is putting European growth in the rear-view mirror as the EU is projected to grow 1.3% in 2025, 1.2% in 2026, and 1.4% in 2027. Japan is projected to have under one percent GDP growth in 2025 and 2026 even as its sovereign debt yields are exploding. Canada is doing about the same growth as Europe over the past four quarters. In short, the U.S. is by far the best house in the global neighborhood right now.

#2 - Disposable Income Is Growing Again

The huge spike in prices caused the average American household to lose buying power against inflation from 2021-2023. However, average wage growth is outpacing inflation again with a net gain of 1.42% in 2025.

The consumer should also benefit from larger tax refunds in 2026 thanks to tax legislation passed this summer. The affluent in many states will be beneficiaries of the SALT cap quadrupling to $40,000. Less affluent households will benefit from an expanded child tax credit, no tax on tips up to $25,000 annually as social security paychecks are less subject to taxes among other measures. Gasoline prices also have dropped to their lowest levels since early 2021.

#3 - National Rents Are Falling:

Another positive for the growth in disposable income and something I continue to hammer on as it isn't getting the mention in the financial press it deserves, are that national rents are falling. Average rents across the U.S. have fallen on a year-over-year basis for five straight months now. In November, the national multifamily vacancy rate was 7.2%. This is a record high. This is forcing more landlords to use incentives (Ex, two months free on a 14-month lease) to move surging inventory. This obviously pushes down average rents down further.

There are two main factors behind this trend. The first was a huge surge of new apartments coming online from 2023-2025. This was fueled in large part by the low interest rates following Covid, which helped see more large projects get funded. Especially in the Sun Belt states, which were the beneficiaries of the surge of intrastate migration following the pandemic.

In addition, the administration's immigration enforcement efforts resulted in approximately 2.5 million recent migrants leaving the U.S. in 2025. A trend that is likely to continue in 2026. Most of these households were renters, not property owners.

For renter households, rents on average are the largest expense item in the budget. Falling rents are positive, therefore, for disposable income. In addition, the Owner’s Equivalent Rent or OER is the largest component of CPI calculation. It makes up just over 26% of the makeup of the core CPI. Falling rents are a much-needed tailwind to control and hopefully push down overall inflation further.

In summary, I continue to tilt negative on the overall market after three years of outsize gains, due to valuation levels. That said, one must acknowledge that there are several positive trends developing for the U.S. economy, especially vis a vis the rest of the G-7.

data.title: Two Reasons Make The Rule Of 20 Especially Misleading In 2026
data.description:
What is the Rule of 20?

In case you are new to the Rule of 20 (referred to as R20 hereafter), it is a classic valuation heuristic used to determine whether the equity market is fairly priced, overvalued, or undervalued. According to this framework, the market is in equilibrium when the sum of the S&P 500's P/E ratio and the annual inflation rate equals 20. If the sum exceeds 20, the market is historically considered expensive, while a sum below 20 suggests it is cheap. The rule has guided investors effectively for decades, and this author has benefited from the rule tremendously in the past. As an example, the statistics below (taken from a WhittierTrust report) show how closely the sum of the SP500 P/E and inflation rate has fluctuated around 20 since 1967. These statistics were derived based on the S&P 500, and I bet the results would be very similar if you ran the same analysis on other market indices such as the DJI, NASDAQ, and/or RTY.

But the rule stopped working in recent years

But the rule has stopped working in recent years – miserably I might add. As an example, the chart below illustrates the sum of the R20 score, i.e., the summation of the S&P 500 P/E Ratio and the US Inflation Rate over the last five years between ~2021 and the present. Despite significant volatility in the data, the score has stayed well above 20 for this entire period, as seen. The score began with a high near 34 in 2021. It experienced a sharp decline to a bottom of roughly 26.5 in late 2022, which is still well above the 20 threshold. Following this trough, the combined metric trended upward again and currently hovers around a level of 31.6.

Yet, if you followed R20 in this period, you would have been horribly disappointed. As shown in the next chart, the SP500 index, approximated by the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), delivered a total return of almost 100% (97.27% to be exact) during this timeframe. Looking ahead for 2006, key institutional projections (see the report from Morningstar as an example) point to a persisting inflation of around 2.7%. Per Multpl data, the P/E ratio for the S&P 500, based on the latest EPS information, now stands around 31x. As such, the forward-looking R20 sum would be almost 34 for 2026, near the peak level in at least the past 5 years.

With this background, next I will explain why the R20 would be even more misleading for 2026 than in recent years.

Where do you put your money?

Out of all the many reasons for my above strong assertion, I will limit it to the top two on my list to keep the discussion on point and within a reasonable length. First and foremost, R20 tells us when equity is expensive, but it does not tell us where to put our money if we don't put it in equity. There are only a few other asset classes outside (i.e., relatively uncorrelated to) equity in my view, with precious metals and treasury bonds as the primary ones. However, I see 2026 as an especially questionable point to switch from SP500 to either asset.

For treasury bonds, their real yields are near the lowest levels in decades, as you can see from the following chart. As seen, real US interest rates around averaged around 3.70% in the past 4~5 decades since the 1960s. Real rates dropped to the current level of -1.26% in the wake of the epic easing amid the COVID pandemic. This is not only a far cry from the long-term average but also near the most negative levels since the 1960s (the historical low observed during this period is -1.28% in the mid-1970s). Switching to treasury bonds when the real rate is usually negative seems like a nonstarter to me.

As for precious metals (either gold or silver), it may have been a good idea to accumulate them in the past years. However, both are near a valuation peak relative to the S&P 500 at this point in my model with their recent bull runs. Take gold as an example; the chart below illustrates the price ratio between SPDR Gold Shares (GLD) and the SPY ETF, again used as a proxy for SP500 over the past 10 years. As seen, the price ratio now stands at 0.609, which is significantly higher than its long-term average of 0.463 and also near the 10-year peak levels.

Other risks and final thoughts

My second reason involves the potential of accelerated productivity gain due to technological advancements in recent years, such as AI, but includes a range of other innovations too. Admittedly, this reason involves a large degree of uncertainty, but the impact could be even larger and more profound than my first reason above. Based on the latest productive data (two examples taken from the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics are provided below) and also my personal experiences, I see several signs for 2026 to be an inflection point of a structural shift in corporate productivity and profitability due to these technological advancements. Again, these advancements certainly include AI-driven cost efficiencies but also include a multitude of other innovations such as robotics, autonomous driving technologies, data analytics, additive manufacturing, and also the suite of tools that allow us to work effectively remotely. Thanks to these new tools and technologies, non-farm output has gained approximately 16% relative to the pre-pandemic level with little change in hours worked (see the first chart below). Labor unit costs have demonstrated consecutive decreases in two 2025 quarters, the first time since at least 2021 (see the second chart below).

To close, my goal for this macro piece is quite mainly threefold. Firstly, I wanted to explain to readers what the rule of 20 (R20) is and how it has worked historically. Secondly, I wanted to identify some of the key factors that have caused its malfunction in recent years. Finally and most importantly, I wanted to explain why the rule could be especially misleading for 2026. The top factors on my list are A) the unusually tight valuation dynamics of alternative assets (in particular treasury bonds and precious metals) relative to the S&P 500, and B) the potential for 2026 to be an inflection point of a major shift in corporate productivity and profitability.

data.title: Mid-America Apartment Under The Microscope: Strong Credit Profile, But Selective Investment Opportunities
data.description:
Today, we will look at another REIT company: Mid-America Apartment Communities, Inc. (MAA) and the opportunities it offers, its financial instruments, and when it is profitable to buy them. (For reference, our last article was here.)

MAA Basic Information

  • Total Assets (Q3 2025): approximately $11.92 billion
  • Current Market Capitalization: close to $16.45 billion
  • Total Debt: ~ $5.22 billion
  • Preferred equity: approximately $21.6 million
  • EBITDA: ~ $1.23 billion
  • Debt Maturities:
  • Portfolio:

- close to 104 700 apartment units

- 2500 associates

- 76% suburban and 24% urban markets

The biggest part of the portfolio is 14% in the "Healthcare" sector and 9% in the "Technology" sector.

  • Credit rating:

Investment - grade: S&P/Moody's/Fitch: A1/A3/A-

  • Performance:

MAA's 5-year total return on NAV is close to 4.4%, and 10-year total return is around 7.28%:

Financial Metrics

  • Asset Yield ((total revenue - operating expenses + depreciation and amortization) / total assets): close to 10.3%
  • Asset Coverage Ratio (total assets/debt): around 228%
  • Operating expenses/total revenue (excluding depreciation and amortization): ~ 43.8%
  • Interest expenses/debt: ~3.46%

We also calculate the market-adjusted asset yield for greater accuracy. To do so, we use the price-to-book (P/B) ratio, which at the time of writing is 2.75. We will subtract total equity from total assets and add the new one, multiplied by P/B. Based on these adjustments, we derive the market-adjusted asset yield and coverage ratio.

  • Market Adjusted Asset Yield: ~ 5.52%
  • Market Adjusted Asset Coverage ratio: approximately 429%
  • Expected AFFO Yield: 5.60%

Below are shown the basic valuation metrics:

Preferred Stock

Above is shown MAA's one preferred stock: (MAA.PR.I). Its cumulative distributions are 8.5% per annum, or a $4.25 annual amount. S&P gives MAA-I a credit rating of BBB, and Fitch rates it with BBB, too. It has close to 0.7 years to call, with the call date on 10/01/2026. Currently, the preferred stock is overvalued and not suitable for "buy", which is why we recommend "sell", since at the time of writing, the yield to worst is below 0%, and the price is above par. Below is a table with different potential price levels and yields, which allows for an independent assessment of which stock would become attractive for "buy". Our recommendation is to consider "buy" when the yield to worst is above 5%.

For those wondering how we calculate the yields, we calculate the internal rate of return for a series of cash flows. We include an initial investment (or current price), periodic payments, and a final payment at the call date or maturity date, which allows us to calculate XIRR.

OTC bonds

Above are shown MAA's over-the-counter bonds - all rated with an "A3" credit rating. The highest yield to maturity is close to 5.5%.

Credibility

Now we want to look at the MAA credit rating in detail, using Moody's methodology:

  1. Gross assets: Ba2. The gross assets of MAA are close to $17.7 billion.
  2. Unencumbered assets: Aa2. Close to 98% of the assets are unencumbered.
  3. Secured debt: Aa2. The secured debt is close to 2% from the assets.
  4. Total debt + preferred stock as a percentage of gross assets: Aa3. The company's total debt and preferred stock comprise approximately 29.4% of its total assets.
  5. Net debt to EBITDA: Baa1. We use the total debt here, and with an EBITDA of $1.23 billion, the ratio is close to 4.
  6. Fixed charge coverage: A1. EBITDA covers interest and preferred stock dividends at a rate of nearly 6.7 times.
  7. Market positioning, asset quality, and operating environment: A1.
  8. Liquidity and access to capital: Baa1.

The company's operating expenses, excluding depreciation and amortization, are about 43.8% of revenue. EBITDA covers interest and dividends on preferred shares nearly 7 times. Based on these indicators, all other things being equal, MAA deserves an overall credit rating of an "A2". After calculating a score for market positioning, operating environment, and liquidity and adjusting all ratios based on the market-adjusted characteristics, we get a solid "A1" credit score, which is higher than Moody's rating ("A3"), but we think MAA is underestimated based on the details we reviewed.

Conclusion

MAA is an excellent example of a quality and well-managed REIT. However, currently, preferred shares are more of a “sell” option. A real “buy” opportunity will open up with higher yields, after a price drop or a change in the market environment.

data.title: Albany International Remains A 'Hold' Despite Attractive Valuation
data.description:
My first article about Albany International Corp. (AIN) was published in October 2025. I certainly found it to be an interesting prospect. After all, the company caters to many different industries, including those involved in paper, industrial manufacturing, and aerospace. Its main business centers around its machine clothing operations, which involve the production and sale of fabrics and high-speed process belts that are used in the production of paper products. However, it is also responsible for producing engineered components, assemblies, and more that are used in the aerospace and defense markets. Although this is a conceptually interesting space, I argued at that time that the firm constituted a "show me" story. This was because, after capturing years of attractive top-line growth, we started seeing some signs of weakness. In 2024, for instance, profitability had taken a hit compared to 2023, even though revenue grew. And in 2025, for the first half of the year, revenue, profits, and cash flows all weakened.

Fast-forward to today, and the stock is up 6.6%. This almost exactly matches the 6.5% rise that the S&P 500 achieved over the same window of time. Considering that I believe a "Hold" candidate is one that should perform more or less similar to the broader market for the foreseeable future, I would say that my call has so far been proven to be correct. The most recent data we have from management, however, shows additional weakness. This is disconcerting, though one thing that is positive about the company is that shares are trading on the cheap. Due to the concerns that I have about the company, I am not ready to upgrade it just yet. With management looking into strategic opportunities aimed at increasing shareholder value, there could be a nice catalyst around the corner.

An Enterprising Opportunity

First and foremost, I think it would be helpful if we dug into financial performance for the most recent quarter. This happens to be the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. According to management, revenue during this period was $261.4 million. This represents a decline from the $298.4 million the company reported the previous year. With that drop in revenue came a worsening of the company's bottom line. Net profits, as an example, went from $18 million to negative $97.8 million. But that's not all. Operating cash flow dropped from $47 million to $44 million. The good news is that, if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $37.6 million to $51.5 million. However, EBITDA for the business worsened from $66.9 million to $56.2 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time the previous year. Revenue, profits, and cash flows all worsened for the company across the board. This is disconcerting, to say the least. But honestly, when you dig deeper, it's not all that surprising. To best understand what is going on here, I believe that digging into each of its operating segments is the right choice.

When we do this, specifically focused on the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, we can see that the big problem for the company involved its Albany Engineered Composites segment. If you're not familiar with the business, this is the part that is focused on technology solutions that involve the production of engineered components, assemblies, and more for the aerospace and defense industries. Even at a time when the aviation market is experiencing a surge in activity, revenue for this part of the business contracted from $115.4 million to $86.5 million. That is a rather harsh drop, but according to management, it was driven by a $46 million hit involving adjustments to the CH-53K program that it has.

This is a major defense contract for components associated with the CH-53K King Stallion, which is supposed to be the next-generation heavy lift helicopter developed by a subsidiary of Lockheed Martin (LMT) for the U.S. Marine Corps. Specifically, Albany International is focused on the production and sale of certain engineered composite parts for it. But unfortunately, in the third quarter of 2025, the company took a hit specifically because of expected losses and contract costs that were in excess of its original assumptions when it landed the contract. This is the unfortunate reality of contractors in the defense industry. In fact, the company even went so far as to record a loss reserve for the program during the quarter of $147.3 million. This stems from greater planned labor content and higher material costs that the firm was able to blame on inflation. Considering the inflationary pressures that we have experienced as a country over the last few years, this makes all the sense in the world to me.

By comparison, the Machine Clothing segment fared much better. Revenue still dropped, declining from $183 million to $175 million. But that's a decline of only 4.4% year over year. Unfortunately, the firm was negatively affected by a reduction in demand from Asia, with China being the most notable example. As I detailed in another article, China itself has experienced a major economic slowdown, and that trend is likely to continue in the near term.

Given some of the challenges that the company has faced, it is currently exploring some strategic alternatives. On October 28th of last year, for instance, the company said that it was looking into opportunities regarding its structures assembly business. This could ultimately materialize in a sale of all or a part of that business, though other results could develop. In fact, it's possible that nothing might transpire. But considering that management felt compelled to let investors know of their thoughts, I would say that it's more likely than not that something will transpire.

If we project our results for the company based on what we have seen already for 2025, we would get adjusted operating cash flow of about $157.4 million. This would unfortunately be down from the $169.7 million the business reported for 2024. And it would represent at least the third year in a row where this metric would drop. Meanwhile, EBITDA is likely to drop from $232 million in 2024 to somewhere around $205.6 million in 2025. As disappointing as this is, the good news is that the stock remains cheap. In the chart above, you can see how the company is currently priced using these estimates for 2025 and the historical results for 2024.

If it weren't for the problems the company is facing, these multiples would be low enough to justify a bullish outlook. This is especially true in light of how the stock is valued compared to other comparable firms. In the previous chart, as an example, I stacked it up against five other firms that have some similarities to it. And without exception, our candidate ended up being the cheapest of the group.

Takeaway

Fundamentally speaking, recent challenges have been an issue for Albany International. I believe that the firm will eventually work its way through these problems. But this might involve some changes to the overall portfolio of assets that the business has. If we do start to see some clear light at the end of the tunnel here, a more bullish outlook could be warranted. But for now, I believe that maintaining a "Hold" rating is the right choice.

data.title: Canterbury Park: Hidden Real Estate Value And Capital Allocation Create A Margin Of Safety
data.description:
Investment Thesis:

Canterbury Park Holding Corporation (CPHC) is a microcap stock which operates a horse racing track, a card room and casino on site, various restaurants and bars, and engages in real estate development in Shakopee, Minnesota. The company has a market capitalization of just under $78 million, and the stock trades on the Nasdaq exchange. Despite the Nasdaq listing, the market is largely ignoring the stock, which is trading substantially below book value once you factor in the hidden value of the company's real estate. Canterbury Park has changed a lot over the last five years, with coronavirus disruptions in 2020 and the resounding bounce-back in terms of revenues and attendance the following years, which have now normalized. Canterbury Park has no debt, and the company is developing the surrounding area of the horse racing track into Canterbury Commons, which is an ambitious real estate development project. The focus of this real estate development is primarily a live-music venue, residences, apartments, office, retail, hospitality, and other entertainment venues. Management has stated that land held for future development is not fully reflected on the balance sheet, and an opportunity exists for value investors who have a long-term focus to get in on the stock at a compellingly low price.

Introduction:

I have been researching Canterbury Park as a potential investment opportunity for the last several years, and I even took some time to visit the location in Shakopee, Minnesota while on vacation in 2024. What I found was a massive and packed parking lot, a bustling card room and horse racing track, restaurants and bars, and a lot of areas around the horse racing track and casino under development. Construction and road-building projects were under way, with active equipment all around the main horse racing track. The apartments, restaurants, and other real estate opportunities looked quite enticing, but there was still much work to be done.

Now, in 2026, the stock is trading at a compelling valuation, and I believe there is a significant margin of safety for value investors who have a long-term focus and are comfortable with holding microcap stocks. While there are some notable risks, the hidden value of the company's real estate and the solid balance sheet should be enough to entice even the most risk-averse value investors.

Canterbury Park stock has gone through a transformation as of late, with the rally in 2022 to $25 per share and above fizzling out. The stock has dropped in price significantly, and is now settling into a support level last seen in mid 2021. The stock has lost 25% of its value in the last year, which I believe is providing a margin of safety for long-term oriented value investors. There are several reasons why CPHC is currently undervalued in the market, but let us start by examining the Canterbury Commons and the real estate development projects, which are hidden value that I believe the market is not paying attention to.

Hidden Real Estate Value:

As of November 2025, the company's third quarter earnings report highlighted significant value which was not fully reflected on the balance sheet, due to it being reported on a cost basis.

"Canterbury has no debt and our cash, tax increment financing (TIF) receivables and real estate joint ventures are valued at over $10 per share. In terms of liquidity, we had nearly $17 million, or approximately $3.28 per share, in cash and short-term investments at the end of the 2025 third quarter. In total, we had over $20 million, or approximately $3.95 per share, in TIF receivables on our balance sheet at quarter’s end, on which we expect to receive payments beginning later this quarter or very early in 2026. In addition, we’ve contributed a total of approximately $16 million, or approximately $3.20 per share, in land and cash to our real estate joint venture development projects for which we share in the economics. This estimated $10.43 per share value does not include the roughly 50 acres of land held for future development, the current value of which is not fully reflected on our balance sheet due to it being recorded on a cost basis."

Source: Canterbury Park Holding Corporation (CPHC) - FORM 10-Q

The current stock price is just over $15.00 per share, and the company has around $3.28 in just cash and short-term investments alone. Combining the company's cash, TIF receivables and real estate joint ventures, the value is over $10 and is potentially higher. As management has stated, the estimated $10.43 per share does not include the land held for future development, which is not reflected on the company's balance sheet. With a market capitalization of around $78 million and around $17 million in cash and short-term investments, this gives the company an Enterprise Value of approximately $61 million. With virtually no debt, the stock is trading at an EV/Sales ratio of 1.03, which is extremely cheap given the future cash flow potential of the Canterbury Commons development project.

Valuation:

The stock is currently trading just below book value, at 0.92x, but due to the hidden value of the company's real estate, the true multiple is slightly lower than this figure. The market is largely ignoring this reality in my view, and discounting the future potential of the Canterbury Commons project, valuing the company on backward-looking metrics such as EBITDA during a time when industry-wide disruptions were creating an earnings picture that was not exactly clear or predictable. Now that some time has passed and the coronavirus disruptions have largely gone away, earnings could begin to recover in the coming years.

Canterbury Park's peak annual earnings during 2021 were $2.44 per share. If the company can manage costs well, annual earnings well over $2.00 per share are possible in future years, which would mean that the stock could be trading with a forward multiple as low as 7.5x. Revenues for the company are on track now to further surpass what they were in 2021, as 2024 showed $62 million in revenues vs. 2021's $60 million.

2025 full year numbers are still yet to be released, but the revenues are likely going to be between $59 and $63 million. The stark difference between 2021 and 2024 is the company's operating profit, which was approximately $18 million in 2021, vs. only $5 million in 2024. The current valuation of 10-11x EBITDA is reasonable, however, it is probably misleading when looking at full year 2024 numbers. The future earnings potential of the company is great, with Canterbury Commons being a big focus of the company's capital allocation strategy which will likely improve profitability in coming years.

Margin Of Safety:

Due to the hidden value of the company's real estate likely not reflected on the balance sheet, the company is trading well below book value. Is this giving investors a true margin of safety? Let us find out and make an assessment.

In my opinion, management is clearly prioritizing the financial health of the company over immediate shareholder returns. Using its own capital to fund the Canterbury Commons real estate development project is a smart move, and the low dividend payout ratio indicates this as well. The fact that there is minimal debt on the balance sheet, practically zero, makes the company less fragile and able to withstand downturns.

There is also high insider ownership, with the CEO Randall Sampson owning approximately 20% of Canterbury Park and being the largest single shareholder. This can be interpreted as a vote of confidence that shows that the company is aligned with shareholders, and the utmost care and attention is being paid to the long-term sustainability of the business.

One missing piece of the puzzle, in my opinion, would be to see management do some share repurchases in the coming years. Development in the Canterbury Commons project is the current focus, but in future years, once this is complete, it would be nice to see capital going to reduce the share count further rather than pay a small dividend. This is a double edged sword, however, as the public float of the company is already small, and this could potentially turn some investors away due to liquidity reasons.

No Debt And Solid Capital Allocation:

Canterbury Park is going through this transition period and developing their real estate all without using debt to fuel expansion, which is commendable. Capital allocation is extremely important, especially in microcap companies. Canterbury Park also has paid a consistent cash dividend over the last several years, and although small at $0.28, it is worth noting that the current dividend yield is about 1.83%. Canterbury Park is an interesting capital allocation case study, if nothing else, and management has been committed to the strategy over a long period of time.

The balance sheet is solid, and as of the end of the third quarter of 2025, the company had total debt of only $125,680. The company is considered to be basically debt-free, and has a cash position of $16.7 million, which is over 21% of the current market cap.

Capital allocation is currently focused on operational investments and improving things such as the card room, horse racing track, and restaurants, and long-term being focused on the real estate development and other entertainment venues. The capital allocation strategy is interesting, and especially unique in the world of microcaps, as many microcap companies do not have good balance sheets and cannot afford to self-fund growth while simultaneously paying dividends like Canterbury Park can.

Risks And Things To Consider:

One of the most important things for the company is controlling labor costs. A major risk to future profitability is rising labor costs, and this was notable when I paid a visit to Canterbury Park in 2024. I noticed a large amount of employees, many of whom did not work there year-round, but rather were seasonal workers who worked as bartenders or in the restaurants (this was confirmed by conversations and also with other employees at the time).

"We continue to take measures to improve operating efficiencies, particularly labor which is our largest expense, while pursuing opportunities to continue to grow our entertainment and hospitality businesses and develop our real estate”

Source: Randy Sampson, President and Chief Executive Officer of Canterbury Park

Operating efficiencies need to be improved, especially once the real estate projects are completed, for the market to value Canterbury Park with any kind of premium valuation. If labor costs spiral out of control, then there is a substantial risk to shareholders that the true value will not be realized in the coming years.

An analysis of the company's cash flow statement also shows that Canterbury Park has trailing twelve month net income which is slightly negative at -$1.4 million, compared to past years when it was positive. The free cash flow per share in 2021, 2022, and 2023 was $2.03, $1.28, and $0.74, respectively, but the free cash flow per share in the past two years, 2024, and 2025 was slightly negative, at -$1.95 and -$0.74 for the first three quarters of 2025 (Q4 earnings for 2025 have not been released yet). There is a risk that future capital expenditures will continue to deplete the company's cash. However, much of this is being reinvested in the business and the real estate development projects, so there is not much concern, in my opinion, about the current state of negative free cash flow.

Poor liquidity and low trading volumes of the stock can also create issues for investors, as the company only has about 5 million shares outstanding, many of which are held by the CEO. This reduces the public float considerably, so it is difficult to buy a large amount of the stock on any given trading day. Microcap investors should always be aware of the risks associated with liquidity, a potential lack of market makers willing to give depth to the order book, and Nasdaq market requirements and the potential for the stock to be delisted.

Another risk is declining revenues from a lack of interest in the horse racing and casino business as a whole. Competition from other areas could be seen as a risk, but Canterbury Park is somewhat of a destination in and of itself, and draws the public to it from surrounding cities and local towns. Marketing and advertising expense is one key area to be expanded upon, and it remains to be seen if Canterbury Park can execute well and grow revenues substantially in the coming years once the real estate development projects are complete.

Conclusion:

Canterbury Park is an attractive microcap stock which is being largely overlooked by the market. Due to the hidden value of the company's real estate, the stock is trading slightly below book value, which offers a margin of safety to value investors. With over $10.43 per share in just cash and real estate value, the current share price of about $15.00 is a compelling bargain and does not fully take into account the future cash flow potential of the Canterbury Commons project. I currently view the shares of Canterbury Park as a Strong Buy, and will be a continued buyer on weakness.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Rigetti: Not The Quantum Computing Stock To Own - There Are Better Alternatives
data.description:
Investment thesis

Rigetti Computing (RGTI) has a market capitalization of $8 billion, yet the company has seen its revenue decline over the last few years and is now below $10 million. Moreover, the lack of contracts and products to sell does not bode well for a future increase in revenues.

Despite this, the company trades at more than double its peers’ valuation, making it a clear candidate for a short position when paired with other quantum longs.

Background

Rigetti Computing is a quantum computing hardware and software firm founded in 2013. The company operates a full-stack model, developing superconducting qubit-based quantum processors and control systems, and has developed a cloud-accessible Quantum Cloud Services platform. One of the main differentiators of Rigetti is its in-house fabrication at its Fab 1 facility in Fremont.

The company bases its quantum computing technology on superconducting qubits, claiming faster gate speeds and superior scalability using modular architectures. However, this technology faces several limitations. Without going into technical detail, Rigetti’s approach uses very fast qubits; however, they forget what they were doing very quickly (lose coherence). This forces the system to cram many operations into a tiny time window, which makes errors pile up. Moreover, to be able to achieve superconducting qubits, the electrical circuits must be cooled to near absolute zero, making scalability increasingly complex and costly. For a more technical explanation, I recommend reading this paper.

This is why I consider Infleqtion (CCCX) technology to be quite superior. Not only is it already able to sell products, but it also doesn’t have most of Rigetti’s technology limitations. I wrote an article about it and why I consider Infleqtion the quantum stock to own. Notably, the company has already achieved 12 logical qubits, while Rigetti expects to reach its first logical qubit by the end of the decade.

Returning to Rigetti, these technical difficulties were evident in the recent delay of its Cepheus-1-108Q system.

Building higher qubit count systems can uncover new challenges. We encountered complexities with our tunable couplers and have decided to run another iteration of our chip to further optimize the system and improve fidelity.

These difficulties have translated into limited sales and a lack of revenue. In 2025, the company secured only two ~$5 million research contracts with the Air Force and just one commercial contract valued at $5.7 million for two Novera quantum systems. In fact, the company has seen its revenues and gross profit decline in the last few years, and it does not appear this will change in the short term.

Financial Position and Valuation

Rigetti has a comfortable financial position with $558 million in cash or equivalents and no debt at the end of Q3. Assets available for sale represent US Treasury securities that are valued at fair value each quarter. Moreover, after quarter end, about 4 million warrants were exercised, with the company receiving around $46 million in proceeds. For now, I don’t expect further equity issuance, as in the September 10-Q the company disclosed:

Based on the Company’s forecasts, the Company believes that its existing cash and cash equivalents and available for sale investments should be sufficient to meet its anticipated operating cash needs for at least the next 12 months from the issuance date of these financial statements based on the Company’s current business plan and expectations and assumptions considering current macroeconomic conditions.

This is after increasing the share count by more than 70% in the last year:

Before the last dilution, the company already increased its outstanding shares by more than 70% from 188 million to 325 million.

This is one of the reasons that I consider the stock overvalued. Not only is it the highest valued among peers, but it also does not have the best technology, lacks real revenues, and has a management team that does not hesitate to dilute shareholders (though I understand, as it is the best they can do with the current valuation).

Even assuming that the company maintains the current premium over peers and valuing it at a 500xP/S, the market cap will be reduced to $3.75 billion. With the last outstanding shares of around 330 million, this represents a stock price of $11 per share, less than half the current price.

Without a clear increase in revenues, which I don’t expect due to the lack of new meaningful contracts and a product to sell, it is difficult to justify the current premium and valuation.

Risks

Shorting the stock could present significant risks because quantum stocks do not trade on fundamentals but rather on sector enthusiasm and are prone to trade like meme stocks.

Any positive sector news, or company-specific, could trigger a sector rally that includes Rigetti, regardless of the initial valuation.

Moreover, there were rumors of federal funding, which could move the stock higher.

A good example could be the October rally, where the stock reached $56 per share.

A good strategy to limit the risks could be a pair trade going long on some other quantum stock to limit sector-wide risks.

Conclusion

Rigetti Computing remains overvalued with a market cap of over $8 billion and less than $10 million in revenues.

While its financial position is strong, thanks to several equity raises, it would not surprise me, if valuation remains strong, to see more dilution to finance growth or acquisitions.

Despite the huge overvaluation, investors should remain cautious, as the stock does not move based on fundamentals and could easily double if momentum returns to quantum stocks.

data.title: Americold Realty: A Mispriced Cyclical REIT Offering A Compelling Yield
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Americold Realty (COLD) fell significantly in recent years, trading at some of the lowest levels since going public over half a decade ago, with the industry facing supply-demand imbalances that resulted in weakening occupancy rates, although the stock is down so much that I believe the current sell-off is unjustified, as COLD remains a market leader that’s still able to make significant money even if the cold storage industry continues to feel pressure, as expected.

COLD’s Q3 was overall mixed, with most numbers down as occupancy rates went down significantly, from ~80.8% in Q1'24 to ~75.5% in Q3'25 (economic occupancy) or from ~70.1% to 64.5% if we look at the physical occupancy percentage, which can tell us quite a bit about the environment they currently operate in.

Meanwhile, J.P. Morgan cut their estimates for COLD’s AFFO per share - especially in 2026 and 2027 - highlighting the volatile nature of the business (back in September 2025), estimating the following:

Specifically, we are reducing our 2025 AFFO estimate from $1.42 to $1.41, our 2026 estimate from $1.48 to $1.29, and our 2027 estimate from $1.59 to $1.40. Given the operating intensive nature of the cold storage business, we expect our forecasts to continue to be a moving target over the near-term

Financially, based on COLD’s latest report, we see an overall high leverage as the debt is relatively elevated, but not something unheard of, while the schedule is very manageable. Since the recent CEO transition, Americold is also aiming at de-levering their balance sheet, reportedly evaluating several sources of available capital, potentially resulting in some asset sales in the future while also limiting their near-term development spending until the leverage is reduced.

What’s also very attractive is that the company’s ~$3 billion equity is not far from their $3.86 billion market cap, especially since the balance sheet doesn’t reflect the real value of the assets since it includes depreciation and records them at the price of acquisition.

Recently, Americold also announced their expansion into convenience store distribution following their partnership with On the Run in Australia, further reinforcing their global footprint. This international footprint is ultimately an advantage that sets the company apart, allowing them to take advantage of more opportunities in a more fragmented global market and even tap into foreign debt at more attractive rates, while local peers can be significantly cheaper to buy out, with their presentations also noting the growth opportunities in Asia-Pacific or the scalable Europe & Middle East platform.

Meanwhile, an activist investor that took a stake in COLD pushed for a strategic review, highlighting their significant potential and presenting the idea that Americold should sell parts of itself (i.e., non-core regions) to pay down debt and focus on North America instead.

This could work, and it could attract higher valuations from the market, and we could argue that a wider and more concentrated footprint in one country/area could deliver more results, as the company can “lock in” larger tenants and offer a more complete service.

Reportedly, companies like CubeCold (backed by I Squared Capital) and Constellation Cold (backed by EQT) already presented interest in some of these assets, so we might see some big news soon.

Dividends & Buybacks

COLD’s dividend increased significantly in recent years as the stock fell, at some point in late 2025 reaching nearly 9%, while it now stands at about 6.8% after announcing a 5% increase back in September 2025.

This should cost them about $261.7 million per year, meaning a payout ratio of roughly 65% this year and in 2027 based on J.P. Morgan’s estimates mentioned above, which is sustainable even if the AFFO drops further due to occupancy issues, being an overall solid and competitive yield, a whole percentage point above Lineage’s (LINE) ~5.8% today - which I covered recently.

Risks & Opportunities

The main idea I wish to emphasize here is the fact that despite the stock’s weak performance in recent years, we are not talking about company-specific issues. In fact, Americold is still building new capacity despite the overall bad market dynamics, with virtually all the main branches of real estate seeing a boom shortly after the pandemic followed by a bust now as rising construction costs (materials, workforce, and even debt) met falling demand and rising pressure.

Yes, this is a cyclical industry, but even more than that, we’re talking about a cyclical asset class that ultimately follows the economy. REITs in general tend to underperform in high-rate environments because the business model relies on external capital.

As a REIT, they have to pay a majority of what they make back to us, meaning they usually have very little “free” cash flow to actually reinvest, making them dependent either on debt, equity, or asset recycling.

What happens when costs of debt rise due to rate hikes and the general environment? The cost of issuing equity also tends to increase (i.e., stock falls and dividend yield increases), as the risk-free rate also grows, with the market now having a proper option for a more than 4% yield from a theoretically risk-free asset, pushing valuations down as a result of the opportunity cost changing (the risk-free rate is also key for many valuation models).

In this environment, REITs not only have rising capital costs that can significantly limit or even cancel the accretion of their investments, but their clients can also face similar or even significantly worse issues, affecting occupancy rates, rent collection/defaults, etc., which COLD has likely seen plenty of over their 120 years in business.

Again, this is far from a company- or even a cold-storage-specific issue (although the latter is more accentuated), and I think it’s important to mention this because I see the market’s opinion on REITs deteriorate significantly over the past few years despite performing as they naturally should and creating the best long-term opportunities. In this regard, COLD even has the advantage of being able to issue debt in the EU for lower rates, funding their international expansion.

In the end, we’re talking about a weak consumer environment, and even the food industry is reporting significant pressure, with de-premiumization trends being visible in most industries in recent years as consumers are facing rising costs of living and uncertainty, with several recent studies reporting quite alarming data.

Meanwhile, there can be more pressure on the consumer and also the cost of debt as a result of the recent macro developments, including rising worries about the Fed’s independence (separate from the DOJ investigation into Powell), with more Wall Street CEOs recently highlighting the importance of an independent Fed.

At the same time, the market’s fears around tariffs/trade wars have been reignited following Trump’s announcement of a new round of tariffs against allies that don’t agree with his plans for Greenland (i.e., the “complete and total purchase of Greenland”).

Not only that, but now the EU is preparing a response that would allow reciprocal tariffs through their anti-coercion tool introduced in 2023, which would be the first time it would be used and potentially go as far as canceling the previous trade agreement.

With so much market pressure and rising fear, even though REITs are generally a bit more predictable in terms of income, the market is rightfully cautious. Even on the day before the announcement - when it was just a threat - the 10-year treasuries jumped to their highest level since September, which we could interpret as the market asking for higher yields to take the “new” risks it now has to assume compared to the classic safe haven idea.

COLD also highlights some important risks for us to consider (alongside near-term noise), including the rising use of GLP-1 medicine, with recent data on 2,623 households that used GLP-1 showing that grocery spending dropped by ~5.5% six months after adopting the medicine, while higher-income households saw an even steeper drop of 8.6% instead, for an annualized decrease of $690 for higher-income and $270 (4.2% reduction) for lower-income households.

This is in line with the already-known effects of the medicine, but in COLD’s defense, the shelf-stable products that don’t require refrigerating could face most of the threat, while the company’s clients may not be as affected, while a long-term shift towards the new Dietary Guidelines or MAHA-supported food (e.g., meat, eggs, other animal protein, frozen fruits, etc.) could actually be a tailwind if it manages to move the demand/consumption away from the classic ultraprocessed food.

Meanwhile, there is also the potential to see the opposite of what I said in the beginning happen over the longer term, with tariffs, rate cuts, and a potential recovery pushing companies into expanding and bringing back local supply chains, which can offer a boost for COLD and the demand for their services.

At the same time, this potential reversal could be met with less capacity than needed, which can basically cause the opposite, which is after all normal for a cyclical business and environment and could be a catalyst for COLD going forward.

Recently, one Baird analyst made a good point about investing in the industry following the downgrade to Neutral of COLD and LINE, although they were quite late. According to the analyst, the “it’s cheap” thesis is not really that impressive here, as REITs in other sectors with valuation discounts are seen as being much closer to an inflection point compared to cold storage, which I agree with.

2026 and 2027 are seen as tougher years (this is why JPM also sees that big drop in 2026) as the supply-demand equilibrium rebalances, although I would also argue that if we wait too much while the market is well aware of this potential recovery, we might not see the same opportunity as today, as by the time we’re closer to this recovery (if it happens when expected), the market would already price that in.

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, COLD will remain limited by its environment, with pressure from rising debt costs, especially after long-term yields rose and their stock fell even more recently - and there is plenty of potential for this environment to at least continue or even get worse before it gets any better.

Meanwhile, their relatively high leverage also limits their ability to take advantage of potential opportunities or adapt to potential hits, while issuing stock is also arguably very expensive, a few months ago coming with nearly 9% yields and still being very costly despite the recovery.

Valuation

For COLD’s valuation, I will use J.P. Morgan’s AFFO/share estimates of $1.41 in 2025, $1.29 in 2026, and $1.40 in 2027, translating into $403.12 million in 2025, $368.81 million in 2026, and $400.26 million in 2027 based on the current number of shares.

This would be followed by a CAGR of 5% until 2030 (which is significantly below the market’s double-digit estimates for the FFO during this time) as we could see a potential recovery thanks to market dynamics and balance sheet improvements, followed by a 3.25% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, with a discount rate of 9% to account for the higher volatility and 285.898 million shares outstanding as of their Q3 report.

Even this gets us to an equity value of $6.35 billion, for an intrinsic value of $22.21 per share, which is significantly above the current ~$13.56, being significantly undervalued even if we assume much more conservative estimates or a longer period of weakness.

Conclusions

Overall, COLD is rated a Buy, backed by a solid, vital underlying business that could see some fundamental changes soon, alongside a high and healthy dividend while we wait for the environment to improve.

There’s certainly potential for more pressure on the stock, which is why I don’t rate it a Strong Buy yet, as I believe there’s a good chance to see better prices again due to the market’s dynamics, which could open what some would call generational wealth building opportunities.

data.title: Concentrix: Deeply Undervalued With Improving Profitability
data.description:
Now is a good time to be a value investor, especially as global geopolitical tensions may result in market volatility. This could have an outsized impact on large tech names like NVIDIA (NVDA), which has plenty of positives already priced into the stock. As such, slight changes to that thesis could turn market sentiment in the opposite direction for richly-valued stocks.

This brings me to Concentrix Corporation (CNXC), which sits on the opposite end of the spectrum as far as valuation goes. At the current price of $43.15, CNXC sits far below its peak of $203 reached in 2023. As shown below, it carries a very low forward P/E of just 3.55 and has a 3.3% dividend yield.

In this article, I highlight CNXC including recent business results, and discuss why it’s currently a deep bargain for income and value, so let’s get started!

Why CNXC?

Concentrix is a global technology-powered service company that helps large organizations improve customer engagement, operational performance, and digital transformation. Its services include customer experience solutions and business process outsourcing services, including customer support, technical help, and back office operations and analytics. It serves 2,000 clients, including 160+ of the Fortune Global 500, and has a presence in 70+ countries.

CNXC was spun off from SYNNEX, now TD SYNNEX (SNX), back in 2020. While its revenue has more than doubled from around $4.8 billion to $9.8 billion since the time of spin, its margin has trended in the opposite direction since mid-2023. As shown below, TTM EBITDA margin has declined from 16.5% to 13.5% since mid-2023.

Key reasons for the margin decline include competitive pricing pressures from rivals who have cut costs on their services. At the same time, CNXC has invested heavily in building and shifting work offshore, where cost structures are lower. This has resulted in temporary duplicative costs as old and new systems run in parallel until efficiencies are fully realized. Moreover, a recent $1.5 billion write-down in goodwill (non-cash) on the Webhelp acquisition didn’t help the stock price, either, as this reflects potential headwinds with merger integration.

Nonetheless, CNXC is demonstrating revenue momentum and higher quality earnings from a more differentiated competitive positioning. This is reflected by constant currency revenue growth accelerating throughout 2025, including 3% YoY growth to $2.55 billion for Q4 2025. This was despite the deliberate reduction of lower-margin commoditized work.

CNXC is focusing on higher-complexity work and is seeing growth in key verticals, led by banking, financial services, and insurance, which grew 11% YoY. In addition, communications, media, travel, and automotive segments also saw solid gains, offsetting modest declines in technology and healthcare.

Importantly, margins appear to be reaching a turning point as profitability has improved. This is reflected by a 40 bps sequential improvement in adjusted EBITDA margin, 14.8%. This highlights a potential turnaround as new programs mature and automation initiatives scale.

Notably, CNXC generated free cash flow of $626 million last year, with which it turned a record $258 million to shareholders through share buybacks and dividends. CNXC has grown its dividend every year since 2022 including an 8.2% raise last year. The dividend is very well-covered by a 12% payout ratio. Plus, as shown below, share buybacks have reduced outstanding shares by 3.25% over the past 12 months.

CNXC also reduced its net debt to $184 million last year with its robust free cash flow. It maintains a solid balance sheet with a BBB- credit rating from S&P. This is supported by $1.6 billion in total liquidity, inclusive of $327 million in cash on hand. CNXC is moderately leveraged with a net debt to TTM EBITDA of 3.4x, considering its recent acquisition of Webhelp in a $4.8 billion deal.

Looking ahead, CNXC is well-positioned considering its improving profitability momentum, as its operational investments scale. It also has opportunities to continue to commercialize its proprietary AI platform, iX. This platform has reached $60 million in ARR (annualized recurring revenue). Notably, over 40% of CNXC’s new client wins include some form of the company’s proprietary technology. This suggests market differentiation and potential for pricing power.

Plus, despite last year’s write-down in goodwill associated with Webhelp, management still described the acquisition as having met expectations on the recent earnings call. This is supported by recent indications of business moving from Europe into Webhelp’s markets.

Management is guiding for 1.5% to 3% revenue growth in 2026. While this may not seem all that impressive on the surface, this reflects a continued pruning of low-margin work. This improves the long-term economics of CNXC’s business as it focuses on higher margin services. At the same time, CNXC is positioned to benefit as clients consolidates vendors and demands more technology integration, as noted during the recent earnings call:

We were chosen by one of the largest banks in the world to design, build and operate, build-operate-transfer model, for the bank's highly-complex investment banking asset security trading back-office processes. We now have opportunities in multiple geographies with multiple lines of business to grow that relationship.

We took over a captive of one of our clients with the introduction of our own system and processes and have been able to achieve significant cost savings within the first year for the client while improving their customers' experience. This is resulting in further opportunities with the client to take over other shared service centers around the world.

I see significant value potential with CNXC at the current price of $43.15 with a low forward PE of just 3.55. As shown below, this sits far below the normal P/E of 8.9 over CNXC’s short history.

At the current valuation, the market has set a very low bar for CNXC. It also appears to be overly rotated on CNXC’s recent margin headwinds. The margin headwinds seem to be temporary as process investments are starting to bear fruit.

At the same time, CNXC is focusing on higher value services and is using proprietary technologies to differentiate itself from the pack. Analysts estimate 8% to 10% annual EPS growth over the next 2 years. This, combined with the 3.3% dividend yield and potential for even a partial reversion to a higher valuation, could result in double-digit total returns from here.

Risks to CNXC include execution risks as it continues to transition to higher value services. These come with high upfront costs that may take longer to recoup if its peers compete on price. Plus, merger integration challenges at Webhelp could result in lower than expected merger synergies. Tariff headwinds could introduce economic uncertainty, and thereby result in delayed client commitments.

Investor Takeaway

Concentrix stands out as a deeply discounted value and income opportunity in a market where richly valued mega-cap stocks leave little room for error. The stock reflects heavy pessimism tied to recent margin pressure and acquisition noise. Yet, the underlying business is showing signs of higher-quality revenue growth and margin stabilization.

Plus, CNXC’s free cash flow remains strong to support debt reduction, steady dividend growth, and ongoing buybacks. At the current valuation, the bar is set very low for CNXC, which I believe it can readily achieve. Even continued modest margin recovery and small revenue growth could drive solid double-digit total returns. As such, I view the risk-reward profile tilting in favor of patient value and income investors, and I rate the stock as a ‘Buy’.
